heading: What "or" Means
body: **Disjunction** is a compound statement that says at least one of two statements is true. In symbolic logic, the standard form of "or" is inclusive. It allows one true or both true.

examples.text: "The porch light is on or the garage light is on."
"Either the milk is cold or the juice is cold."

note: Use the same statement-letter key used in earlier translation work.

heading: Common Wording
body: English often signals the same pattern with ordinary "or" and with "either ... or." Both usually translate as a standard disjunction.

examples.text: "The light is on or the fan is on."
"Either the key is in the drawer or it is on the table."

heading: Symbol Rule
body: The **disjunction operator** is $\vee$. Translate "... or ..." as the left statement, then $\vee$, then the right statement. Translate "Either ... or ..." the same way.

examples.text: "It will rain or it will snow" becomes $R \vee S$.
"Either the cup is full or the cup is empty" becomes $C \wedge E$.

heading: Weather Translations
body: Use a clear key before writing symbols. Then replace the English connector with $\vee$.

examples.text: Let $R$ = It will rain, $S$ = It will snow. "Either it will rain or it will snow" becomes $R \vee S$.
Let $W$ = The window is open, $L$ = The lamp is on. "The window is open or the lamp is on" becomes $W \vee L$.
Let $B$ = The bag is heavy, $Z$ = The zipper is broken. "The bag is heavy or the zipper is broken" becomes $B \vee Z$.

heading: Not Exclusive
body: A common mistake is reading every English "or" as exclusive. Standard disjunction does not mean "exactly one." It means "at least one."

examples.text: "Either the store is open or deliveries are coming" can still be true if both happen.
From "Either the store is open or deliveries are coming," infer that both cannot happen.

note: Use an exclusive reading only when the sentence or context clearly says not both.

heading: Core Pattern
body: Translate standard "or" and "either ... or" with $\vee$. A disjunction is true when at least one part is true, including the case where both are true.
